metadata.title: Constraint Builder: Close the Fabrication Gap

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want the '[uncertain]' variant because I need the summary to keep moving but still surface where Claude left the documents behind. For my three-report summary: (1) write the full evidence constraint that limits Claude to only the provided sources; (2) add the rule that any ungroundable claim gets tagged [uncertain] rather than dropped; (3) show me how the marked output would look on a statistic that appears in none of the three reports, so the boundary is actually visible.

props.command: I want the '[uncertain]' variant because I need the summary to keep moving but still surface where Claude left the documents behind. For my three-report summary: (1) write the full evidence constraint that limits Claude to only the provided sources; (2) add the rule that any ungroundable claim gets tagged [uncertain] rather than dropped; (3) show me how the marked output would look on a statistic that appears in none of the three reports, so the boundary is actually visible.
props.segment: Constraint Builder: Close the Fabrication Gap

beats.narration_text: The prompt asked for a summary of the three reports. There were no constraints. Claude read the reports and produced a confident, professional summary — with two statistics and one finding that appeared in none of the three documents. Not because Claude tried to fabricate. Because the prompt did not say: use only the provided sources. Without that constraint, Claude filled the gaps from training. The output looked complete. It was not grounded. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, summarize three reports, no constraint

props.command: summarize the key findings from these three reports

beats.narration_text: A prompt without an evidence constraint does not fail — it produces coherent output. Claude generates text that is statistically plausible given its training and the context provided. When the evidence is thin, Claude fills in with what it knows. The fill-in is dressed in the same professional prose as the grounded content. Nothing in the output marks the boundary between what came from the documents and what came from training.
beats.new_visual_element: Fabrication gap — no visible boundary between grounded and training-sourced content

beats.narration_text: The evidence constraint names the boundary explicitly. 'Use only the three reports below.' 'If a fact is not in the provided materials, do not include it.' 'If you would need to guess, say I do not have a source for this and stop.' Each version draws a line and tells Claude what to do when it reaches that line. The constraint does not prevent fabrication — it makes fabrication visible by forcing Claude to stop rather than fill.
beats.new_visual_element: Evidence constraint forms — three variants: hard stop / name the gap / flag uncertain
beats.spark_line: Name the boundary. Force the stop.

beats.narration_text: The most useful variant for professional work keeps the output moving. 'If you are uncertain about a claim, mark it with [uncertain] and I will verify it.' Claude does not stop — it signals. The output continues, but every claim Claude cannot ground in the provided sources carries a flag. The review step becomes targeted: check the flagged claims first. That is faster than verifying the whole document.
beats.new_visual_element: Signal variant — [uncertain] tag keeps workflow moving / review targets flagged claims

beats.narration_text: Constraints do not remove the need for review — they make review tractable. A constrained output is inspectable: you can check each claim against the provided sources. An unconstrained output is not inspectable in the same way — you do not know which parts to check because nothing marks the gap. The test of a good constraint is whether it makes the output easier to audit. If it does, it is the right constraint.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — constrained output: inspectable / unconstrained: gap invisible

beats.narration_text: Add an evidence constraint to your next summary task. Paste this.

props.command: Summarize the key findings from the documents below. Evidence constraint: use only the provided documents as sources. If a specific fact, figure, or name is not in these documents, do not include it. If you are uncertain whether a claim is grounded in the documents, mark it [uncertain] — do not fill from your training. [paste documents here]

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Constraints do not remove the need for review — they make review tractable. A constrained output is inspectable: you can check each claim against the provided sources. An unconstrained output is not inspectable in the same way — you do not know which parts to check because nothing marks the gap. The test of a good constraint is whether it makes the output easier to audit. If it does, it is the right constraint. Add an evidence constraint to your next summary task. Paste this.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Constraint Builder: Close the Fabrication Gap] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
props.segment: Constraint Builder: Close the Fabrication Gap
